comments.title: My Take on Revival Druidry and What Makes It Last
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I hope everyone is having a wonderful week and a great summer (or winter, for folks down under)!

To Share: I've written up a very short introduction to the Druid Revival, interwoven with how it came to be a part of my own spiritual path. Likely not much this crowd doesn't know about what Druidry is or where it came from, but hopefully there might be some interest in what it has meant to me: https://jpowellrussell.com/#a_quick_grounding_in_revival_druidry

To Ask: I've been thinking through what it is that has allowed the Druidry Revival to quietly keep on chugging for 300 years without ever riding the wave, up and down, of being an alternative religion fad like Spiritualism or Theosophy. I've come up with a list of the things I think have done the most, but I'd welcome thoughts from both JMG and the commentariat on whether these are a. right and b. exhaustive. So, challenges to my assumptions as well as things that I am clearly missing are most welcome! Stated briefly, I think that the Druid Revival has been able to stick around so long, comfortable out on the fringe for the following reasons:
1. It spoke to a real unmet spiritual need of the early modern period that continues to be inadequately met by mainstream options: engaging with the divine through nature
2. A very appealing central aesthetic image: the widely (but barely) known druids of yore
3. Around this very clear spiritual and aesthetic core, druidry is /very/ flexible, allowing it to easily fit into some rather different spiritual lives and different times
4. Druidry has emphasized "walking the walk" - don't just put out dogmatic statements, try to lead your life such that it's consistent with what you wish the world were more like
5. All of these things have been done out in the open - any time folks who aren't on board have looked at Druids doing their thing, they can clearly see there's nothing wicked going on, at worst it's some weirdos doing their own thing peaceably.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: My Take on Revival Druidry and What Makes It Last
replies.content: Those certainly did play roles, but -- er, Revival Druidry did in fact go through a series of booms and busts. The largest of those was in the early to mid-19th century, when quite a few people in Britain went cuckoo for Druidry. We were spared the most recent round of boom-and-bust activity, the one that saw Wicca go up with the rocket and down with the stick, mostly because Revival Druids had a reputation for being dull!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Oh my, that's an embarrassing misstep, I either wasn't aware of or had forgotten about the 19th century boom for Druidry. Thanks for clearing that up!

replies.title: Re: My Take on Revival Druidry and What Makes It Last
replies.content: So, that would mean a certain amount of dullness and a relative lack of razzamatazz is actually a survival advantage for a religion, because such a religion attracts only those more determined and those not dependent on fast results? A fitting comparison in this case would be with inconspicuous perennial plants.

replies.title: Re: My Take on Revival Druidry and What Makes It Last
replies.content: That's a good general rule. Anything that makes a tradition attractive to pop culture means that it'll get a rush of members once it becomes fashionable, but those new members will head off for other pastures as soon as some new fad hits. If it's quiet, dowdy, and (especially) if it requires regular personal practice, it's got a much higher possibility of being around for the long term.

comments.title: Re: The Hartlib Circle!
comments.content:
It was tolerably common for magical systems in the Renaissance to also have cryptographic dimensions; Trithemius's book Steganographia is both a handbook of ciphers and a guide to summoning spirits, and plenty of Cabalistic methods can be used for both purposes.

You might want to have a look at Thomas Rudd as a potential link between Dee and the Hartlib circle, for that matter.

replies.title: LaVey & Parsons featured on Americas Untold Stories
replies.content:
If I may...

LeVey claimed to have dropped out of school and joined the Clyde Beatty Circus where he became a calliope player. I'm not sure if its true though, because it is hard to know what are lies from someone who is a Satanist. I heard the same things about his organ and synth playing. For a nebbish he managed to get in league with the Hollywood Babylon crowd by his antics.

The YouTube podcast America's Untold Stories did an episode on him, but I haven't listened to this one. (I got into a heated exchange with one of the hosts on their locals channel, which I am a member, when I had to defend Aleister Crowley, for better or worse, after their episode on Jack Parson's - so I decided to skip this one. Mark Groubert's work on the figures surrounding the JFK assasination however is top notch, and they cover a lot of American people who really deserve to be better known -or cover people known, but show sides of them that have been untouched by others. Getting these people from America's history out into wider circulation is something I appreciate, hence my support of their show, despite some quibbles over the occult. I guess that's to be expected from time to time, howeever.) Here is the LaVey showfor those who are interested:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1nXRb6FfTnY

And here is the Jack Parson's show:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=H4hm_oyKw4k&t=3s

My quibble was they called Parson's and Crowley Satanist's -not accurate in my view. The episode titled Satan and Rockets would have been better titled as Love and Rockets (as the band of the same name gave a nod to Parsons) and been more accurate. That said, they did a pretty good job on him, despite the satan smears. The highlight for me was when Mark Groubert laid out a theory that the explosion that killed Parson's may not have been an accident on his own part (after all, Parson's had been a rocket scientist and familiar with the dangerous materials he had to work for some time), but an assassination in its own right (this part starting around the 50 minute mark). His lover, muse and magical partner Marjorie Cameron never believed that it had been an accident, nor did his scientist friends. Both knew how careful he had been.

One of the thoughts that comes out of this, is America could have had a rocket & space program developed soley through our own scientists, instead of those that came over from Operation Paperclip -and the moral rot that started to spread through the government when they brought people like Von Braun over.

So there is my plug for America's Untold Stories -others here may enjoy some of the episodes, and I think focusing on these interesting characters from our collective past is a good way to build up & strengthen a collective American identity, that paradoxically is in part built around individuality.

https://www.youtube.com/@AmericasUntoldStories/streams

Hail Johnny Appleseed!

Justin Patrick Moore

Hi JMG,

You have sometimes advised that people with mental illness severe enough to be prescribed antidepressants etc. shouldn't study ceremonial magic. Is this because of the risks presented by the features of such illnesses themselves, concerns about the interactions between the effects of magical energies on the body and the effects of medications or other issues?

If a practising magician fell so ill as to require such medication, would you recommend they modify their practices?

I realise this is dicey territory, so I emphasise that I am not requesting advice about the use or prescription of any medication, just about what you think a magician could or should do in the situation I've described.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Antidepressants are among the most overprescribed medications in America today; people who are suffering from ordinary, reasonable levels of depression and anxiety are routinely put on them. A mage who is put on antidepressants for a condition that would not have been medicated fifty years ago can probably continue practices without difficulty, though I'd recommend regular invocations of Venus and Jupiter to help balance out the emotional state. It's only in the case of really serious clinical depression -- the sort that makes everyday life activities impossible -- that it's probably wise to step back a bit on one's practices.

Interestingly, Marsilio Ficino back in the Renaissance considered melancholia (that is to say, depression) an occupational hazard of scholars and mages, and recommended invocations of the Sun, Venus, and Jupiter, along with warming and moistening foods and plenty of music, to help balance the emotional state.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) By "invocations of the sun and planets" I mean magical rituals, performed every week in the day and hour of the relevant planet, to call down the influence of that planet into your personality.

2) By "warming and moistening foods" I'm referring to the Western tradition of the four humors; Stephen Taylor's The Humoral Herbal is a fine introduction to that tradition. Melancholy is cold and dry, so foods that are energetically warm and moist will help balance them.

3) I never said antidepressants weren't warranted in some cases! They're apparently very valuable for people who do have serious clinical depression; they're just massively overprescribed.

4) Yes, it's one of the four humors, but in excess it produces the illness of melancholia.

comments.title: solstice and equinox
comments.content:
Hello JMG and everyone here,

What happens on the spiritual plane during the solstice and equinox?

thanks you,

replies.title: Re: solstice and equinox
replies.content: The solstices and equinoxes are special points in the interactions between the Solar Logos, the spiritual being "who hath made his tabernacle in the Sun," and the planetary spirit of Earth. At the equinoxes, when the earth's orbit crosses the plane of the ecliptic, a current of influence from the sun energizes the earth. At the solstices, when the earth is as far as she goes from the plane of the ecliptic, the earth turns back to renew her relationship with the sun. The resulting energetic changes are important in many traditions of ceremonial magic.

replies.title: Re: solstice and equinox
replies.content: Is that why people who are sensitive feel the equinoxes and solstices? I know I do. Those times make my body and brain whacky. Any suggestions on dealing with the energetic changes?

replies.title: Re: solstice and equinox
replies.content: That's one of the things that ritual celebrations of the solstices and equinoxes are for. They help the mind shift to the incoming influence, and they also help some of the excess energy ground out.

"Elixir :a Parisian perfume house and the quest for the secret of life" by Theresa Levitt.

"A story of alchemy in Bohemian Paris, where two scientific outcasts discovered a fundamental distinction between natural and synthetic chemicals that inaugurated an enduring scientific mystery.

For centuries, scientists believed that living matter possessed a special quality―a spirit or essence―that differentiated it from nonliving matter. But by the nineteenth century, the scientific consensus was that the building blocks of one were identical to the building blocks of the other. Elixir tells the story of two young chemists who were not convinced, and how their work rewrote the boundary between life and nonlife.

In the 1830s, Édouard Laugier and Auguste Laurent were working in Laugier Père et Fils, the oldest perfume house in Paris. By day they prepared the perfumery’s revitalizing elixirs and rejuvenating eaux, drawing on alchemical traditions that equated a plant’s vitality with its aroma. In their spare time they hunted the vital force that promised to reveal the secret to life itself. Their ideas, roundly condemned by established chemists, led to the discovery of structural differences between naturally occurring molecules and their synthetic counterparts, even when the molecules were chemically identical.

Scientists still can’t explain this anomaly, but it may point to critical insights concerning the origins of life on Earth. Rich in sparks and smells, brimming with eccentric characters, experimental daring, and the romance of the Bohemian salon, Elixir is a fascinating cultural and scientific history."

comments.title: zeeo and human sanity
comments.content:
JMG and commentariat - Looking at platonic solids and geometry I came across an interesting proposition regarding the Cistercians and the introduction of zero:

*****

They claimed zero was a device of the devil.

This may seem silly to our modern minds, but they realized the profound implications zero would have on human consciousness that would open the door for humanity to separate itself from its spirituality and believe that the material reality was the only true reality.

Merchants adopted the zero, nonetheless, for ease of calculation.

Suddenly zero permitted numbers to represent ideas which have no form. This was an entirely new way of looking at numbers."

In antiquity, ‘idea‘ meant ‘form‘ and implied geometry and Idealism represented consciousness giving form to reality.

‘Idea‘, therefore, was redefined. Ideas became less real than physical ‘things’. Humanity began to become separated from the knowledge that consciousness (ideas/thoughts/emotions) formed all physical reality.

Lawlor explains, "This breakdown of the simple, natural logic of the arithmetic structure allowed a complicated mental logic to take its place and invited into mathematics a whole range of numerical and symbolic entities, some of which have no verifiable concept or geometric form behind them."

Physicist Conrad Ranzan writes, “The rejection of aether and the consequential incomplete theory of gravity has led theorists to propose highly speculative universes of mathematical genre – abstractions devoid of reality.”

Dr. Harold Aspden concurs, “Modern physical theory has become abstract. The starting points of the original papers on the subject are mathematical, the treatment is mathematical and the conclusions are mathematical. In many instances there seems to be no relation whatsoever to the phenomena which make up the world of experimental physics.”

*****

Two more quotes from the article:

"Culturally speaking, the reverse of this ‘spiritual nihilism’ of the East is ‘materialism’ of the West."

"The ancients knew consciousness was the source of the physical. This began to slip away during the ‘Age of Reason’."

Has anyone read or seen discussion of these ideas?

I am finding the idea that introduction of zero could have initiated a cascading effect that has resulted in western societies level of current craziness quite fascinating - that the shift from the monad representing unity by introducing a level of abstraction via zero, has quite literally, driven folks insane?

Any thoughts?

For anyone interested, here is the link: https://www.cosmic-core.org/free/number-zero-and-one/

Given zeroes and ones under all this digital malarkey - if the cistercians were correct, it might go some way to explaining some of the creepiness of the internet.

Might we be needing the quivlent of a butlerian jihad before the human species can regain some semblance of balance and harmony?

Cheers all

[earthworm]

replies.title: Re: zeeo and human sanity
replies.content:
I first encountered that same proposal in Robert Lawlor's fine book Sacred Geometry: Philosophy and Practice. It's quite correct, of course.

There are at least two other consequences of the introduction of zero that might be worth considering here. First, it ruined the West's sense of proportion. The number line used to go like this

(nothing) .....1/4, 1/3, 1/2, 1, 2, 3, 4..... (infinity)

This made it natural and easy to think of four as one-fourth of a whole, and so on; this fostered a way of thinking about proportions that makes good art much easier. Replace it with:

(negative infinity???) ....-4, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, .... (infinity)

Which fosters the modern habit of thinking that you can just add on more units indefinitely, and thus leads to ugly art and architecture.

But the second one's the real kicker. Care to guess why negative numbers became so popular in late Renaissance Europe? They made it easy to calculate debt. The entire bizarre modern conception of debt as something with its own value, something that can be bought and sold and used as money, rests on negative numbers. In earlier times, people knew better; that's why lending money at interest used to be considered a mortal sin. Dante puts moneylenders deeper in the Inferno even than heretics.

Appendix C: The Incorporation of the Bank of England

In the late 17th century, during the early years of King William and Queen Mary's reign, many projects to establish a bank were presented to the English Government. They were remarkable in that they proposed that the capital stock of an incorporated bank should constitute a permanent, funded loan to the Government. Incorporation was a prerogative of the Crown, which the sovereign exercised by consent to Acts of Parliament.

Motivated by the urgent need to improve the credit of the Government and raise finance for the war against France (War of the League of Augsburg), Government and Parliament agreed to the proposals of Charles Montagu, who drew from an earlier scheme outlined by William Patterson in 1691, a Scottish banker, trader and promoter of the failed Darien scheme. The Bank of England was duly authorised and incorporated by a Ways and Means Act of Parliament (5 and 6 Will. & Mary, c.20) on 27 July 1694, forming “The Governor and Company of the Banke of England”. The first Governor of the Bank was Sir John Houblon and the seal of the corporation was chosen as Britannia sitting atop a bank of money.

The Ways and Means Act of 1694 went by the long-winded title of:

"An Act for granting to theire Majesties severall Rates and Duties upon Tunnage of Shipps and Vessells and upon Beere Ale and other Liquors for secureing certaine Recompenses and Advantages in the said Act mentioned to such Persons as shall voluntarily advance the summe of [£1,500,000] towards the carrying on the Warr against France"

Its principal objective was to bring together public creditors to form a limited-liability, joint-stock company. A sum equivalent to the Bank’s total capital stock would be paid into the "Receipte of Exchequer" in Bank of England assignable bills that the Exchequer would then re-assign in order to pay its creditors. In return, the Bank would receive Exchequer tallies as a record of receipt.
The assignable bills would form a permanent loan to the Government, the interest on which would be paid by way of an annuity. A yearly sum of £96,000, 8% interest on the loan, was to be annually appropriated by the levying of certain taxes and duties (Ways and Means) for payment to the company by installment on a quarterly basis. The company also charged an annual management fee of £4,000, bringing the perpetual annuity due to £100,000. In return, the Act was to grant privileges to the company to conduct the business of banking.
*****

All thanks to a little zero ;)

replies.title: Re: zeeo and human sanity
replies.content:
The other interesting thing is that, mathematically, 0 acts in many respects like an infinity in a way no other finite number does.

Zero times anything except infinity is zero. Infinity times anything except zero is infinity. Zero times infinity and infinity times zero are indeterminate. Anything except zero divided by zero is "undefined", but let's be real; it's infinity. Anything except infinity divided by infinity is zero. Zero to the zero power is indeterminate, etc.

No other finite number breaks mathematics in this way. Negative numbers don't do it. Complex numbers don't do it. Zero is the reason you can't travel faster than the speed of light (if you tried to do so, you'd end up dividing your mass by zero).

comments.content: Since upthreads there is a discussion about why Druidry managed to quietly survive the ca. 300 years since its emergence, my question today is why the Neopagan movement has had such a colorful rise and fall during the last decades? And, presumably, Neopaganmis didn't cease to exist, instead it merely retreated to small subcultures, is this correct?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a complex story. Until the 1970s Wicca was a small and fairly quiet initiatory tradition that required a great deal of work from prospective members. What happened in the 1970s is that a number of people figured out how to create a Wicca Lite that involved a lot less hard work and would be much more appealing to the pop culture of the time. That launched the modern Neopagan movement on its way. Like every form of pop-culture occultism -- Theosophy and Spiritualism are two good examples -- it attracted a lot of people, ballooned in size and influence, got watered down even further, and finally got supplanted by other fads. Now it's on its way back down to small subcultures; it's not quite there yet but the writing's on the wall. If things follow the usual pattern, we'll go through thirty or forty years in which political fads are more popular than spiritual fads before the next pop-culture occultism gets going.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Could you explain how you define the term "Neopagan"? I always took it to mean simply "modern pagan," as in non-Christian earth-based spirituality. So it would include Wicca but many other practices too. But you seem to mean specific groups or types of practice when you use it. And it seems like a pejorative term.

Thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't mean it as a pejorative term, merely as a descriptive term. The usage I have in mind was introduced by the late Isaac Bonewits, who differentiated Paleopagan (i.e., ancient pagan), Mesopagan (i.e., new or revived pagan traditions dating from before the second half of the 20th century), and Neopagan (i.e., new or revived pagan traditions dating from Bonewits's own time). Though he had a polemic purpose in all this, and used "Mesopagan" as a term of abuse, he wasn't wrong that there's a distinction; the late 20th century revival and reinvention of paganism had its own very distinctive character, distinct from Mesopagan traditions such as the 18th- and 19th-century Druid Revival.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Our host has written in more detail on this topic a few times, if you want to dig in a little more:

https://www.ecosophia.net/blogs-and-essays/the-well-of-galabes/the-twilight-of-the-neopagan-era/
https://www.ecosophia.net/blogs-and-essays/the-well-of-galabes/a-wind-that-tastes-of-ashes/
https://www.ecosophia.net/an-older-tradition/

The very short version is that from the 80s through the 00s, a lot of folks started calling themselves "Neopagans" and having Neopagan festivals and what have you. While initially meant to just be a term for "folks who are pagan today and haven't always been," the scene developed its own flavor, as scenes will do. Most folks who embraced the term leaned towards an eclectic approach, and since it was an alternative spirituality scene that made one of its selling points how different from Christianity it was, it tended to skew politically liberal. The political side of things really took off in the teens (like everywhere else), and so prominent neopagan folks and organizations began to take explicit stances on political issues and to expect members to agree on those issues and so forth. The term "neopagan" came to more and more refer to those folks who remained a part of this scene and continued to identify this way, and to a lesser degree, "pagan" has that coloring, at least in the US.

A related, but slightly distinct point is that some folks did/do identify as neopagan who do not believe in the actual reality of the Gods - they see them as archetypes or useful shared images or the like (this is sometimes called "soft polytheism," but that term can also mean other things). Due to its big-tent ethos, the neopagan scene tended to welcome these folks. Since they have an explicitly materialist viewpoint, they often don't get along as well with those polytheists for whom the actual reality of the Gods called upon is important.

Many polytheists who either never were a part of the neopagan scene, or else otherwise felt distinct from it, took a look at the way the scene was going and decided they didn't want to use the label. Many heathens, for example, don't think of themselves as neopagans and don't like being lumped in with the folks who do use the label these days, even though most do fit the letter of Bonewits's definition.

Once you've finished Learning Ritual Magic, I recommend working through at least the first half of Circles of Power while studying Inside a Magical Lodge. comments.title: Feng Shui-ed
comments.content:
Hi! I asked last Monday about remedying the bad vibe of my bedroom, and got some helpful advice:

No feng shui pros in my area, so I did the best I could: read a book on it, as well as the "House that Cleans Itself" book that someone recommended also (despite being a book about housecleaning, she has some very cogent advice about furniture placement)... then digested all that for a bit, moved all my furniture, added a couple of strategic houseplants, and the difference is AMAZING. In case it helps anybody else, I *think* the thing that made the biggest difference was moving the bed-- it had been under a window *and* while not exactly opposite the door, it was the biggest thing in my sightline when I opened the door-- moving it to an interior wall changed both of those things, so now I no longer feel like I'm being irradiated by the streetlights while trying to sleep, and the first thing I see when I open the door is a long stretch of empty floor... which makes the room look way bigger and less crowded. Plants are helpful, but they seem to be a distant third to just moving the bed.

One interesting side-effect: for lack of anywhere else to put it, my desk, and computer, are in my bedroom. I was initially inclined to blame them for the gross vibes, but for whatever reason, moving them to a different wall completely changed how I interact with my computer-- I spend less time at it, and the internet got way more boring. I call that a win! In case it's relevant and anybody else wants to experiment, I moved it from a south wall to an east wall. Both interior walls.

I'm now very curious-- has anybody else found that the location of the computer in your house affects how much you use it, and how engrossed/compulsive you are about using it?

replies.title: Re: Feng Shui-ed
replies.content: Delighted to hear this. Yes, I tend to be very careful where I put my computers -- I have one I used to access the internet, and another with no internet access where I do my writing -- for that reason among others.

comments.title: Golden Dawn, earth magic and Temple technology
comments.content:
Hello, and thank you for providing this forum as always. There's been some talk here about adapting transformation rituals from the CGD to the DMH system, but I'm more curious about the other way around, which I haven't seen discussed much. Invisibility doesn't interest me all that much personally, while I find the idea of helping to heal environmental damage and bring soil fertility a very appealing, or even noble, purpose for practical magic.

1) Would it be possible to do anything like the land healing/blessing rituals using GD methods? In an earlier comment thread you said that fertility magic like the DMH's standing stone working couldn't be done with a purely solar system IIRC, but could you do any kind of "land" magic with the GD system at all? Would this be a dead-end without the Lunar current? The solstice/equinox ritual does seem to have some nods towards blessing the land.

2) In brief, what's the relationship between the "Temple technology" and the fertility magic from the DMH? I'll admit I haven't gotten to your Temple books yet, but in one of your recent podcasts you were talking about your hypothesis that this technology involved microwave radiation (if I understood it correctly). That surprised me, since I assumed these ancient structures were built to create the Lunar current rather than work with physical radiation. Does this lost technology do the same thing on the physical plane that the DMH workings do on the other planes, or am I getting this all wrong? Again, I understand that I'll need to read the book(s) for the full discussion, and I plan on doing that eventually.

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn, earth magic and Temple technology
replies.content:
1) Yes. You'd have to have a sufficiently solid grasp of Golden Dawn magic to be able to create a major ritual from scratch, but given that, yes, there are things you could definitely do.

2) They're two different things. The temple technology is a physical technology; it plays well with magical and spiritual practices, but the core theory of my temple books is that something physical rather than metaphysical is involved in all those old legends of temples bringing increased fertility to the land.

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn, earth magic and Temple technology
replies.content: Not saying that you should join the Druidical Order of the Golden Dawn,  but they do have a ritual for healing the land that is not in the CGD book.

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn, earth magic and Temple technology
replies.content:
2) I thought the idea was supposed to be that the relevant currents were mostly etheric, and most situations where electromagnetic phenomena can be transmitted are also situations where some etheric phenomena can also be transmitted, so that the denser ethers are "physical" in that sense. It sounds to me like, from what people say, the denser ethers more resemble a materialist conception of the substance of the universe, and the subtler ethers resemble an idealist conception more, but it's a matter of degree.

(With enough experience with condensed-matter physics and computer science, one can start to see how that materialism-idealism binary could turn out to be more of a continuum axis. Although, in that framing, the "materialism" side of the binary is still privileged in some more ultimate sense. To borrow the terms of Yudkowsky's thought experiment where you examine a dryad to see what more fundamental processes she is made of, this framing has the dryad being made by the composition of more fundamental processes that ultimately regress to something that's in some way more mechanism-like the way matter is, rather than being made of an unending series of layers of more fundamental processes that continue to be obstinately mind-like, such as "fabric of desire" or "cloth of belief", no matter how many layers of reduction you look through. But then I guess I myself actually am expecting that the supernatural will prove to be "material" in this limited sense; however, that limited sense might not be practically relevant, since the supernatural might easily be complex enough that it would be impossible to work out the supposed underlying mechanisms, and it might easily be even more unexpectedly contextual and subjectivity-dependent than the human relation to a quantum-mechanical universe, or to an interpersonal relationship, or to one's own neurochemistry and neuroanatomy, or to the events of a fever-dream.)

I've mentioned this here before, but haven't looked into it myself: The Wikipedia page on the Kaluza-Klein interpretation of electromagnetism mentions something of a conceptual kluge called the "cylinder condition", which is needed to restrict this otherwise very natural theoretical suggestion for how electromagnetic forces might be an emergent side effect of space-time geometry (the way we already know gravitational forces turned out to be), so that the theory only reproduces the electromagnetic forces we know about. (I don't really understand it, but from what I can follow, the idea of the cylinder condition is metaphorically something like, imagine a hose that can transmit waves of sideways expansion and contraction, and maybe the hose can also transmit waves of pushing or pulling or twisting, but the hose definitely can't transmit any more complicated waves of bunching up around its circumference or bending or anything like that. The cylinder condition says that, for every transverse slice of the hose, all the parts of the hose that are within that same slice are all doing the exact same thing.) There hasn't been much work on what kinds of particle-supporting fields you can also get when you refrain from imposing the cylinder condition, but someone with the right kind of library access (not me) could go look up the work of Paul Wesson, who seems to be the leading authority in this area.

The cylinder condition is interesting because presumably these off-cylindrical fields and particles would be in some ways analogous to electromagnetic fields and photons. So presumably they would be a candidate for a description of dense ethers. However, their relationship to known physics would have to be somehow quite subtle and indirect. For one thing, such particles have never turned up with a distinct signature in particle collision experiments; any effect they've had is aggregate and baked into what other particles are thought to do "by themselves". For another, they don't seem to affect the entropy of electron gases enough for anyone to have noticed, which seems like something you would expect if some ethers are supposedly conducted by conductive materials. AFAIK the distinction between conductive materials and non-conductive materials is a distinctively electronic phenomenon; and my understanding of the relevant thermodynamics is limited, but you'd think that, if ether particles were coupling to electrons strongly enough to be carried or not carried by electron currents, then this would show up somehow in non-exchangeabilities or something and affect entropy measurements. But maybe instead they couple to the electromagnetic field photons themselves, instead of to the electrons, and somehow are then propagated by the fact that the electrons are free to move? And maybe distinctions between etheric field states mostly happen on a scale substantially larger than interatomic distances, and that helps average things out somehow and avoid affecting entropies, and maybe it also prevents them from getting involved in particle collision experiments (which involve much smaller space scales).

Maybe it's worth someone who hypothetically had some relevant kind of actual access to etheric perception doing experiments to see if they can project ethers through various materials that are on the edge of being conductive or non-conductive in different ways, notably sufficiently pure elemental silicon or germanium at different temperatures or dopant concentrations (and what about diodes, especially temperature-measuring diodes?), to see if this edge between conduction and non-conduction falls in different places with respect to etheric conduction than to electronic conduction, or if it really would be all piggybacking on the electron motions.

Metaphorically, if all you were allowed to directly do to a hose was excite it with "cylinder condition"-obeying kinds of forces, it might actually be kind of hard to get the hose to break symmetry and do things like bend or ring. Maybe that's another theory of why etheric phenomena would be hard to access from within an experimental paradigm where you could only control cylinder-condition-obeying particles like electrons. I guess in the case of an actual hose, if you twist it enough, what it does is bend and kink up and fold across its width, but maybe there's no available physical operation that would metaphorically correspond to that. Though, if there was such a physical operation, it would be most likely to look like high-energy particle collision experiments, or possibly high-energy nonlinear optics experiments.

I guess I'm not sure what the point of speculating about this or doing any experiments or whatever would even be, though. Because, I'm expecting that, even if any of this was in some deeper sense "true", still, why wouldn't the universe just end up going to the same efforts to actively prevent anyone anywhere from getting long-term publicly replicable findings in paraphysics experiments as it observably goes to actively prevent anyone anywhere from getting long-term publicly replicable findings in parapsychology experiments? What's to stop two different people's perceptions about e.g. etheric conduction from being only as consistent with each other as two different people's or two different geographically isolated cultures' independent mystical or revelatory experience communications about the ostensibly same metaphysical facts? It seems like it would be impossible to have a relevant "science" or "engineering" as such, in a universe like that. If Reiki practitioners' perceptions can't get past a little kid's randomization and correctly detect at above-chance odds whether the kid's hand is on the opposite side of a box, or even correctly report that they aren't getting any signal, doesn't the whole enterprise seem kind of hopeless?

replies.title: Re: Golden Dawn, earth magic and Temple technology
replies.content: I'm not greatly interested in speculating. My goal is to reconstruct the old temple technology and put it into operation. My hypothesis is that it's a physical effect, not an etheric one, and so should be entirely amenable to testing.

comments.title: Feeling Heat

replies.title: Re: Feeling Heat

the internet used to be a lot more useful for that sort of thing-- somewhere around 2019, all the major platforms did a coordinated throttle of every non-mainstream source of "health" type information, including forums where self-experimenters posted (which could be so useful!), and started redirecting every query that contained any medical-type keywords to the same five or eight extremely bland, extremely pharma-funded, mostly-useless sites: mayoclinic, webmd, livestrong, etc. If you've made any such queries lately you'll recognize them instantly.

comments.title: Request for References Concerning Celtic Religion plus Holy Wells and Clootie Trees
comments.content:
Last week I asked if anyone could recommend references for the Welsh druid pantheon and I took a long shot and ask if anyone could recommend anything concerning Cornish gods / religion prior to Christianity.**

It turns out that this information is not readily available so I decided to see what I could find. I'm not sure it's possible to know the names of any particular Cornish god as the Roman occupation, Christianity and time have conspired to put that history to rest.

To help build an understanding on what Cornish (and Welsh) druid religion was like, I'm asking if anyone can recommend references covering Celtic religion? I'll keep an update concerning research along these lines here:

https://scottyc.dreamwidth.org/387.html

Another way I hope I can derive understanding of any Cornish pantheon or religious belief is by working back from myths, superstitions and folklore that has survived. I discuss a specific holy well in Cornwall and the clootie tree adjoining it here:

https://scottyc.dreamwidth.org/767.html

replies.title: Re: Request for References Concerning Celtic Religion plus Holy Wells and Clootie Trees
replies.content: Keep in mind that there won't have been a coherent pantheon. In Celtic times each tribe had its own gods and goddesses, some of them very local. You might see if you can find any evidence of Roman-British shrines in southwestern Britain; that might be a good source of clues.

replies.title: Re: Request for References Concerning Celtic Religion plus Holy Wells and Clootie Trees
replies.content: Good suggestion.  Afraid that any Roman / British shrine or temple site now has a church on it but well worth looking into.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Two sources that might help you out if you don't already know about them:

1) The Four Branches of the Mabinogi by Will Parker - Obviously Welsh rather than Cornish, but in the process of analyzing the tales in the Mabinogion through multiple lenses (historic, mythic, political allegorical, etc), Parker brings in a lot of comparative evidence from the wider Celtic world, and sometimes talks about the role other lands played mythically for the medieval Welsh (if I remember right, Cornwall was thought to be closer to the mythic underworld, and so charged with magical power). I wish every work of myth I am interested in had a book of commentary/analysis this thorough.

2) Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe by Hilda R. Ellis-Davidson - This work specifically compares and contrasts Germanic and Celtic pre-Christian beliefs and works to highlight borrowings and mutual influence. It seems more focused on what can be said about all Celtic religion and all Germanic religion, and so it might only occasionally refer to specifically Cornish things, but it's quite wide-ranging and it's bibliography might prove useful.

Good luck!
Jeff

Hello Mr. Greer,

replies.title: Re: Awkward question

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

When doing the Lesser Middle Pillar exercise how many times should I do the Cabalistic Cross? Right now I'm doing it in the beginning, after I do the LBRP and again at the very end. Is this proper? Or should I only be doing it at the very beginning and that the very end?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This comes up often enough that I should probably put it in the FAQ.

Ahem. Let us consult the Book of Armaments:

"Three shall be the number of thy Cabalistic Crosses, and the number of thy Cabalistic Crosses shall be three. Unto four shalt thou not Cross Cabalistically, neither shalt thou Cross Cabalistically unto two, except as thou then Crossest Cabalistically unto three. Five is right out."

Yes, you do it once before beginning the LBRP, once after the LBRP and before you start the Middle Pillar, and then once after the Middle Pillar.

"And having Crossed Cabalistically unto three, being the third number, thou hast completed thine exercise, and all who art naughty in thy sight shall be banished. Amen." (Boom.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you!
